ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lentil soup entry (FOODON_03311069); no Turkish-specific red lentil soup entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lentil soup — FoodEx2 composite dish category for legume-based soups
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Turkish red lentil soup code. A00LC (lentil soup) is the closest defensible composite dish anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Turkish red lentil soup or generic lentil soup dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Turkish red lentil soup weighing approximately 300 g, as typically served in a Turkish or kebab restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Soup, lentil, home-prepared (FDC ID 172462) and Lentils, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt (FDC ID 172421)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Turkish red lentil soup entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Soup, lentil, home-prepared (FDC ID 172462) providing energy ~99 kcal, protein 5.6g, fat 2.8g, carbohydrate 14.4g, dietary fibre 3.9g, sodium 214mg per 100g; and Lentils, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt (FDC ID 172421) providing protein 9.0g, dietary fibre 7.9g per 100g. The input GrabFood-derived values (energy 116.67 kcal, protein 6.0g, fat 4.0g, carbohydrate 15.0g, dietary fibre 5.0g, sodium 233mg, saturated fat 0.67g) are broadly consistent with a restaurant-prepared version of USDA lentil soup with added olive oil and spices, and have been retained as they fall within the plausible range for a Turkish restaurant preparation. Cholesterol 0.0mg and trans fat 0.0g are confirmed zero for a fully plant-based vegan lentil preparation per USDA SR Legacy data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Red lentils, boiled (GI=26, glucose reference).
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Turkish red lentil soup as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 26 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for red lentils, boiled (GI=26, glucose reference scale), as reported in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. This peer-reviewed publication is an approved GI source. Red lentils are the primary carbohydrate-contributing ingredient in this soup, making boiled red lentils the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per 300g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (26 × 45.0) / 100 = 11.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 233 mg/100g; approximately 700 mg per 300g serving
hypertension.guidance: A 300g serving delivers approximately 700 mg sodium, representing 30–35% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertension management; patients should request reduced-salt preparation and avoid adding condiments to keep total meal sodium within target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (26), high dietary fibre 5.0g/100g, moderate carbohydrate 15.0g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index of 26 and high dietary fibre content of 5.0g/100g significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response, making Turkish red lentil soup a suitable choice for type 2 diabetes management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat 0.67g/100g, zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre 5.0g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The negligible saturated fat (0.67g/100g), absence of trans fat and cholesterol, and high dietary fibre (5.0g/100g) support a cardioprotective dietary pattern; however, the sodium content of 233mg/100g warrants attention for patients with concurrent hypertension and CVD risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients (olive oil, garlic, cumin); nightshade ingredients (tomato paste, paprika)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red lentils and olive oil provide anti-inflammatory dietary fibre and oleocanthal polyphenols respectively; however, RA patients with solanine sensitivity should monitor symptoms when consuming tomato paste and paprika, which are nightshade-derived ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Turkish Red Lentil Soup, known in Turkish as Kırmızı Mercimek Çorbası and commonly referred to as Dal or Dhal in South Asian and Middle Eastern culinary contexts, is a smooth, velvety legume-based soup prepared by simmering red lentils with onion, garlic, tomato paste, olive oil, cumin, and paprika until fully softened, then blending to a creamy consistency. It is a staple of Turkish cuisine and is widely served in Turkish, kebab, and shawarma restaurants across Singapore. The soup is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. Nutritionally, a 100g serving provides approximately 117 kcal, 6g protein, 4g fat (of which only 0.67g is saturated), 15g carbohydrate, and 5g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is low at approximately 26, making it highly suitable for blood glucose management. The high fibre content supports gut health, satiety, and cardiovascular protection. Sodium at 233mg/100g is moderate and may be a consideration for hypertension patients in a full 300g bowl. The dish is iron-rich (1.33mg/100g) and provides 20mg calcium per 100g. It is an excellent protein source for plant-based diets and is widely recommended in clinical nutrition for diabetes, cardiovascular disease prevention, and weight management.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of dal?
What is the GI of lentil soup?
How much protein is in lentil soup?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Turkish red lentil soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lentil soup (FDC ID 172462) and cooked red lentils (FDC ID 172421), which are broadly consistent with the input GrabFood-derived values (confidence 0.7). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled red lentils as the closest defensible analogous food, supported by the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 publication in Diabetes Care. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Turkish restaurant-prepared red lentil soup are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lentil soup entry (FDC ID 172462) or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero for a fully plant-based vegan lentil preparation per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for lentil soup and cooked red lentils — no animal-derived ingredients are present. 3. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lentil soup (FDC ID 172462) and cooked red lentils (FDC ID 172421); the input GrabFood-derived values were broadly consistent with these approved sources and have been retained as they fall within the plausible range for a Turkish restaurant preparation with added olive oil and spices. 4. GI of 26 is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled red lentils (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283) as the closest defensible analogous food; no direct GI measurement for Turkish red lentil soup as a composite dish was found in any approved GI source. 5. This dish is served in a Singapore Turkish/kebab/shawarma restaurant context; multilingual names are provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual requirements, in addition to the Turkish romanized name Kırmızı Mercimek Çorbası. 6. Aliases self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language values appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
